revisions.boundaryRationale: The run began in May and private non-support liabilities were still declining through August 29; the permanent program implemented in September and the subsequent orderly markets provide the first explicit stabilization evidence after the revised stress window.

evidenceAssertions.summary: The FDIC archive records the May 1984 intervention and the systemic implications of Continental Illinois for other banks and financial markets.

evidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
evidenceAssertions.summary: Daily liability data show the May run, renewed declines in late June and July, and continued but more modest non-support liability outflows through August 29.

evidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
evidenceAssertions.summary: The permanent assistance plan was implemented in September; borrowings then declined as private market funding recovered and financial markets remained orderly.

counterEvidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
counterEvidenceAssertions.summary: Government support appears to have prevented serious spillovers to other institutions, limiting the breadth of the banking-credit episode despite the prolonged run at Continental.

revisions.boundaryRationale: The peso break starts the repricing in December; Mexican short rates peaked in mid-March, while the peso, rates and equity prices recovered materially in April, making March the last supported stress month rather than an assumed endpoint.

evidenceAssertions.summary: The BIS records heavy Mexican outflows, sharp repricing and regional reverberations after the December peso crisis.

evidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
evidenceAssertions.summary: FOMC minutes document the peso depreciation, spillovers to other developing economies and pressure on the dollar after the December break.

evidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
evidenceAssertions.summary: The Greenbook records a mid-March peak in Mexican Treasury-bill rates followed by a 15 percent peso appreciation in early April and a reversal of more than two-thirds of the equity decline.

counterEvidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
counterEvidenceAssertions.summary: U.S. stock prices, debt spreads and credit availability remained broadly supportive, limiting the breadth of the Mexican repricing episode in U.S. markets.

revisions.boundaryRationale: Trade effects began after the December peso break, remained visible through the second quarter, and later evidence shows that total U.S. exports rose in that quarter despite declines to Mexico; June therefore closes the documented cross-border adjustment window.

evidenceAssertions.summary: The BIS records Mexican capital outflows and regional transmission following the peso crisis.

evidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
evidenceAssertions.summary: The FOMC records lower U.S. exports, higher imports and concentrated effects on trade flows and business activity in states bordering Mexico.

evidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
evidenceAssertions.summary: The Greenbook reports a sharp decline in exports to Mexico and expects the remaining net-export adjustment in the second quarter before a positive contribution resumes.

evidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
evidenceAssertions.summary: Later FOMC minutes show that total U.S. exports rose considerably in the second quarter despite declines in shipments to Mexico, bounding the broader growth transmission by June.

counterEvidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
counterEvidenceAssertions.summary: The FOMC expected Mexico to interrupt only briefly the broader uptrend in U.S. exports because growth among other trading partners remained robust.

revisions.boundaryRationale: The comparison starts with the December peso break and ends with the first quarter of 1995, for which directly reviewable FDIC results show strong U.S. bank earnings and record commercial-loan growth while Federal Reserve evidence confirms continued loan funding.

evidenceAssertions.summary: The directly reviewable first-quarter 1995 QBP reports 11.1 billion dollars of bank earnings, 97 percent profitability and record quarterly commercial-loan growth.

evidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
evidenceAssertions.summary: March FOMC minutes report that banks continued relying on managed liabilities to fund loan growth and that bank lending policies remained accommodative.

counterEvidenceAssertions.provider: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
counterEvidenceAssertions.summary: The peso crisis spread to other developing economies and the dollar, so the hard negative is limited to U.S. banking credit and does not assert universal containment.
